entries.tutorialTitle: Air fryer vs oven: the energy comparison
entries.tutorialSubtitle: When a small appliance saves electricity and when the maths doesn't support the switch
entries.tutorialExcerpt: An air fryer uses 800-1800 W for 10-20 minutes to cook a portion. A fan oven uses 2000-3500 W for 20-40 minutes. For single portions, the air fryer consistently wins: 0.1-0.3 kWh vs 0.4-1.2 kWh. For larger loads or dishes that need even heat distribution, the oven is more efficient per kg of food cooked. This is how to calculate your own comparison.

entries.tutorialTitle: Composting options for flats, balconies, and small urban spaces
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Comparing bokashi, wormeries, and small cold bins for space-constrained households
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Flat-dwellers and those with no outdoor space can still divert kitchen waste from landfill. Three systems work without a garden: bokashi fermentation (a sealed bucket for any food waste), a wormery (a stacked box system for vegetable scraps), and a small cold-composting bin suitable for a balcony. entries.tutorialTitle: Breathable insulation for pre-1919 solid-wall buildings
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Hemp, wood fibre, and lime plaster as alternatives to PIR for older homes
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Solid-wall homes built before 1919 use lime mortar and unpainted brick or stone that absorbs and releases moisture. Sealing them with PIR board and a vapour barrier traps moisture and causes accelerated decay. Breathable insulation materials let the wall continue to buffer moisture while cutting heat loss by 30-50%. This explains the difference and how to choose the right material.

entries.tutorialTitle: Insulating a cellar wall from the inside
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Damp membrane and rigid board insulation for a below-grade basement or cellar
entries.tutorialExcerpt: An uninsulated cellar wall loses heat year-round and often suffers from penetrating damp. Installing a cavity drain membrane followed by rigid board insulation and a timber or metal stud lining transforms an unusable space and cuts heat loss through the below-grade walls by 60-75%. This is the sequence for a dry cellar conversion.

entries.tutorialTitle: Recognising and remediating a failing cold-deck flat roof
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Condensation, rot, and insulation failure in cold-deck construction, and what to do about it
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Most UK flat roofs built before 2000 are cold-deck construction: insulation sits between the joists below the deck boards, leaving a ventilated space above.
